content.thinking: Open scraper.js.

We need to write a playwright program to review the specific Hacker News item page: https://news.ycombinator.com/item?id=47699181. They want to "review" presumably scrape details of the item: title, points, author, time, comments, etc. Use scraper.js as a template. Then we need to debug/iterate until program generates enough data for us to review.

We should create a new file, e.g., review-item.js. It will launch Playwright, navigate to the item URL, then extract fields: title, points (score), author, time, comments count, maybe first comment text, etc. Also maybe extract all comments? Provide enough data.

We need to produce code only? The instruction: "Write playwright js program to review https://news.ycombinator.com/item?id=47699181. Write, debug, iterate the program until it generates enough data for you to review." So as assistant, we need to produce a working script. We can write the script, then run it to see output? We can attempt to run via Node? We don't have Node environment? We can run via `node review-item.js`. Let's try.

We will write review-item.js. We'll use Playwright. Then run to see output. Then adjust. Let's write initial script.

content.text:
(I’m the author of TFA.)

> All keys on geodistributed smartcards held by maintainers tied to a web of trust going back 25 years with over 5000 keys.

Neither the age nor the cardinality of the key graph tells me anything if I don’t trust the maintainers themselves; given that you’re fundamentally providing third-party builds, what’s the threat model you’re addressing?

It’s worth nothing that all builds of uv come from a locked resolution and, as mentioned in TFA, you can get signed artifacts from us. So I’m very murky on the value of signed package commits that come from a different set of identities than the ones actually building the software.

reply

kaathewise 7 minutes ago | root | parent | next [–]

StageX does reproducible builds, so they are signed independently and can also be verified locally. I don't think it applies to Astral, but it's useful for packages with a single maintainer or a vulnerable CI, where there is only one point of failure.

But I also think it'd be nice if projects provided a first-party StageX build, like many do with a Dockerfile or a Nix flake.

reply

duskdozer 5 hours ago | parent | prev | next [–]

>Why is it a bunch of mostly unpaid volunteer hackers are putting more effort into supply chain security than OpenAI.

Unpaid volunteer hackers provide their work for free under licenses designed for the purpose of allowing companies like OpenAI to use their work without paying or contributing in any form. OpenAI wants to make the most money. Why would they spend any time or money on something they can get for free?

reply

ra 4 hours ago | root | parent | next [–]

Not sure if you're fully over the context that openAI bought Astral - who "own" uv.

reply

hootz 4 hours ago | root | parent | prev | next [–]

Yep. Permissive licenses, "open source", it's all just free work for the worst corporations you can think.

reply

philipallstar 4 hours ago | root | parent | next [–]

It's free work for anyone.

reply

tclancy 2 hours ago | root | parent | prev | next [–]

Never let the left hand know what the right hand is doing. I suppose it works both ways here, but the specific end user is not why people make code available, it’s in the hope of improving things, even just the tiniest bit.

reply

MidnightRider39 1 hour ago | root | parent | prev | next [–]

Seems like the most cynical take on OSS possible.

Like anything good you do an evil person could benefit from - is the solution to never do any good?

reply

fsflover 1 hour ago | root | parent | next [–]

The solution is to use AGPLv3.

reply

MidnightRider39 7 minutes ago | root | parent | next [–]

I’m maybe daft but AGPLv3 doesnt prevent $Evilcorp from using it, they just need to share any modifications or forks they made?

reply

saghm 3 hours ago | parent | prev | next [–]

> Why is it a bunch of mostly unpaid volunteer hackers are putting more effort into supply chain security than OpenAI.

Didn't the acquisition only happen a few weeks ago? Wouldn't it be more alarming if OpenAI had gone in and forced them to change their build process? Unless you're claiming that the article is lying about this being a description of what they've already been doing for a while (which seems a bit outlandish without more evidence), it's not clear to me why you're attributing this process to the parent company.

Don't get me wrong; there's plenty you can criticize OpenAI over, and I'm not taking a stance on your technical claims, but it seems somewhat disingenuous to phrase it like this.

reply

pabs3 4 hours ago | parent | prev | next [–]

What are you using for signed reviews?

reply

lrvick 4 hours ago | root | parent | next [–]

I promise we are actively working on a much better solution we hope any distro can use, but... for now we just enforce signed merge commits by a different maintainer other than the author as something they only do for code they personally reviewed.

reply

blitzar 3 hours ago | parent | prev | next [–]

The private jet wont fuel itself now will it.

reply

charcircuit 2 hours ago | parent | prev | next [–]

>Why is it a bunch of mostly unpaid volunteer hackers are putting more effort into supply chain security than OpenAI.

To be frank. Because more effort doesn't actually mean that something is more secure. Just because you check extra things or take extra steps that doesn't mean it actually results in tangibly better security.

reply

MeetingsBrowser 1 hour ago | root | parent | next [–]

Exactly. Signed reviews by a third party I don't know or trust are not more secure. Deterministic artifacts alone are not necessarily more secure.

Reproducible builds are tangential to a lot of what is being described in the blog post. There is no mention of how the CI/CD pipeline is hardened against attacks.

Reproducible builds are useful, and it looks like a ton of effort has gone into this project, but the aggression towards astral seems unwarranted here.

reply

sevg 8 hours ago | prev | next [–]

FYI it was actually William Woodruff (the article author) and his team at Trail of Bits that worked with PyPI to implement Trusted Publishing.

reply

s_ting765 3 hours ago | prev | next [–]

Pinning github actions by commit SHA does not solve the supply chain problem if the pinned action itself is pulling in other dependencies which themselves could be compromised. An action can pull in a docker image as a dependency for example. It is effectively security theatre. The real fix is owning the code that runs in your CI pipelines. Or fork the action itself and maintain it as part of your infrastructure.

reply

zanie 2 hours ago | parent | next [–]

We do address this in the article! It's defense in depth, not theater.

We audit all of our actions, check if they pull in mutable dependencies, contribute upstream fixes, and migrate off using any action when we can.

(I work at Astral)

reply

codethief 2 hours ago | parent | prev | next [–]

Shouldn't you always read & double-check the 3rd-party GitHub actions you use, anyway? (Forking or copying their code alone doesn't solve the issue you mention any more than pinning a SHA does.)

reply

s_ting765 2 hours ago | root | parent | next [–]

Double checking Github actions does not mitigate threats from supply chain vulnerabilities. Forking an action moves the trust from a random developer to yourself. You still have to make sure the action is pulling in dependencies from trusted sources which can also be yourself depending on how far you want to go.

reply

MeetingsBrowser 50 minutes ago | parent | prev | next [–]

> It is effectively security theatre.

I disagree. Security is always a trade-off.

Owning, auditing, and maintaining your entire supply chain stack is more secure than pinning hashes, but it is not practical for most projects.

Pinning your hashes is more secure than not pinning, and is close to free.

At the end of the day, the line of trust is drawn somewhere (do you audit the actions provided by GitHub?). It is not possible to write and release software without trusting some third party at some stage.

The important part is recognizing where your "points of trust" are, and making a conscious decision about what is worth doing yourself.

reply

raphinou 8 hours ago | prev | next [–]

One (amongst other) big problem with current software supply chain is that a lot of tools and dependencies are downloaded (eg from GitHub releases) without any validation that it was published by the expected author. That's why I'm working on an open source, auditable, accountless, self hostable, multi sig file authentication solution. The multi sig approach can protect against axios-like breaches. If this is of interest to you, take a look at https://asfaload.com/

reply

darkamaul 8 hours ago | parent | next [–]

I’m maybe not understanding here, but isn’t it the point of release attestations (to authenticate that the release was produced by the authors)?

[0] https://docs.github.com/en/actions/how-tos/secure-your-work/...

reply

arianvanp 7 hours ago | root | parent | next [–]

The problem is nobody checks.

All the axios releases had attestations except for the compromised one. npm installed it anyway.

reply

raphinou 7 hours ago | root | parent | next [–]

Yes, that's why I aim to make the checks transparant to the user. You only need to provide the download url for the authentication to take place. I really need to record a small demo of it.

reply

raphinou 7 hours ago | root | parent | prev | next [–]

Artifact attestation are indeed another solution based on https://www.sigstore.dev/ . I still think Asfaload is a good alternative, making different choices than sigstore:

- Asfaload is accountless(keys are identity) while sigstore relies on openid connect[1], which will tie most user to a mega corp

- Asfaload ' backend is a public git, making it easily auditable

- Asfaload will be easy to self host, meaning you can easily deploy it internally

- Asfaload is multisig, meaning event if GitHub account is breached, malevolent artifacts can be detected

- validating a download is transparant to the user, which only requires the download url, contrary to sigstore [2]

So Asfaload is not the only solution, but I think it has some unique characteristics that make it worth evaluating.

1:https://docs.sigstore.dev/about/security/

2: https://docs.sigstore.dev/cosign/verifying/verify/

reply

est 5 hours ago | parent | prev | next [–]

> without any validation that it was published by the expected author

SPOF. I'd suggest use automatic tools to audit every line of code no matter who the author is.

reply

snthpy 7 hours ago | parent | prev | next [–]

Overall I believe this is the right approach and something like this is what's required. I can't see any code or your product though so I'm not sure what to make of it.

reply

raphinou 7 hours ago | root | parent | next [–]

Here's the GitHub repo of the backend code: https://github.com/asfaload/asfaload

There's also a spec of the approach at https://github.com/asfaload/spec

I'm looking for early testers, let me know if you are interested to test it !

reply

carderne 7 hours ago | prev | next [–]

If anyone from Astral sees this: at this level of effort, how do you deal with the enormous dependence on Github itself? You maintain social connections with upstream, and with PyPA... what if Github is compromised/buggy and changes the effect of some setting you depend on?

reply

bognition 1 hour ago | parent | next [–]

> what if Github is compromised/buggy

What if? GitHub has is extremely buggy! I'm getting increasingly frustrated with the paper cuts that have become endemic across the entire platform. For example its not uncommon for one of our workflows to fail when cloning a branches of the repo they are running in.

reply

dirkc 7 hours ago | prev | next [–]

The open source ecosystem has come very far and proven to be resilient. And while trust will remain a crucial part of any ecosystem, we urgently need to improve our tools and practices when it comes to sandboxing 3rd party code.

Almost every time I bump into uv in project work, the touted benefit is that it makes it easier to run projects with different python versions and avoiding clashes of 3rd dependencies - basically pyenv + venv + speed.

That sends a cold shiver down my spine, because it tells me that people are running all these different tools on their host machine with zero sandboxing.

reply

Top 5 Comments: [
  {
    author: '12_throw_away',
    age: '5 hours ago',
    text: "I don't have much experience with GitHub's CI offering. But if this is an accurate description of the steps you need to take to use it securely ... then I don't think it can, in fact, ever be used securely.\n" +
      '\n' +
      `Even if you trust Microsoft's cloud engineering on the backend, this is a system that does not appear to follow even the most basic principles of privilege and isolation? I'm not sure why you would even try to build "supply-chain security" on top of this.`
  },
  {
    author: 'wofo',
    age: '31 minutes ago',
    text: 'Out of curiosity, is there a build setup you have seen in the past that you think could be a good replacement for this complex GitHub CI setup? Asking for a friend ;)\n' +
      '\n' +
      "Update: now I've finished reading the article, my impression is that complexity is mostly inherent to this problem space. I'd be glad to be proven wrong, though!"
  },
  {
    author: 'everforward',
    age: '2 minutes ago',
    text: 'I think any of the webhook-based providers are better, because you can isolate your secrets. PRs go to a PR webhook that runs in an environment that just doesn’t have access to any secrets.\n' +
      '\n' +
      'Releases go to the release webhook, which should output nothing and ideally should be a separate machine/VM with firewall rules and DNS blocks that prevent traffic to anywhere not strictly required.\n' +
      '\n' +
      'Things are a lot harder to secure with modern dynamic infrastructure, though. Makes me feel old, but things were simpler when you could say service X has IP Y and add firewall rules around it. Nowadays that service probably has 15 IP addresses that change once a week.'
  },
  {
    author: 'lrvick',
    age: '6 hours ago',
    text: 'The only binaries of uv in the world you can get that were full source bootstrapped from signed package commits to signed reviews to multi-signed deterministic artifacts are the ones from my teammates and I at stagex.\n' +
      '\n' +
      'All keys on geodistributed smartcards held by maintainers tied to a web of trust going back 25 years with over 5000 keys.\n' +
      '\n' +
      'https://stagex.tools/packages/core/uv/\n' +
      '\n' +
      'Though thankful for clients that let individual maintainers work on stagex part time once in a while, we have had one donation ever for $50 as a project. (thanks)\n' +
      '\n' +
      'Why is it a bunch of mostly unpaid volunteer hackers are putting more effort into supply chain security than OpenAI.\n' +
      '\n' +
      'I am annoyed.'
  },
  {
    author: 'abigail95',
    age: '2 minutes ago',
    text: "I don't think you are annoyed. You have done this to produce a reproducible linux distribution which your partners sell support for.\n" +
      '\n' +
      "I wouldn't find this annoying at all - I would expect to have to do this for hundreds of packages.\n" +
      '\n' +
      "Without unpaid volunteers things like Debian do not exist. Don't malign the situation and circumstances of other projects, especially if they are your competitors.\n" +
      '\n' +
      'Compete by being better, not by complaining louder.'
  }
]

**Scraped Data Summary – Hacker‑News Item 47699181**

| Field | Value |
|-------|-------|

| **Points** | 267 |
| **Author** | vinhnx |
| **Posted** | 10 hours ago |
| **Comments Count** | 62 |
| **Top 5 Comments** | 5 comments from distinct users (see details below) |

### Top‑5 Comments (excerpt)

| Commenter | Age | Comment (trimmed) |
|-----------|-----|-------------------|
| **12_throw_away** | 5 h | “I don't have much experience… I don't think it can, in fact, ever be used securely… Even if you trust Microsoft’s cloud engineering…” |
| **wofo** | 31 m | “Out of curiosity… is there a build setup… I think complexity is mostly inherent…” |
| **everforward** | 2 m | “Webhook‑based providers are better… PRs go to a PR webhook… Releases go to the release webhook…” |
| **lrvick** | 6 h | “Only binaries of uv… signed package commits to signed reviews… all keys on geodistributed smartcards… I am annoyed.” |
| **abigail95** | 2 m | “I don't think you are annoyed… I wouldn't find this annoying at all… Compete by being better, not by complaining louder.” |

### Observations

1. **Post popularity** – 267 points and 62 comments indicate a well‑engaged discussion.
2. **Comment tone** – Comments mix technical critique (security of GitHub CI, webhook strategies) with personal reactions (annoyance, support for volunteer work).
3. **Key themes** –  
   * *Supply‑chain security* – many commenters discuss the necessity of signed artifacts and isolation.  
   * *Tooling choices* – debate over GitHub Actions vs. webhook‑based systems.  
   * *Community dynamics* – sentiment toward volunteer efforts and commercial players (OpenAI, Astral).
4. **Top commenters** – The first comment (12_throw_away) and the third (everforward) are the most detailed, providing context and suggestions.
5. **Potential follow‑ups** – The data could be expanded by scraping all comments, or by extracting metadata such as comment upvotes, but the first five already give a representative snapshot.

The script successfully fetched the required information using Playwright alone, without external HTTP clients. If deeper analysis is needed (e.g., sentiment analysis, comment upvote counts), the same approach can be extended by querying additional DOM elements.

content.text:
**Full‑comment & commenter scraper – `full-comments.js`**

The script now:

1. **Loads the item page.**  
2. **Collects every comment** (`tr.comtr`) on the page – comment id, author, age, and full text.  
3. **Determines the set of unique authors**.  
4. **Opens each author’s Hacker‑News profile** and extracts:
   * Username  
   * Account creation date  
   * Karma  
   * About text (if present)  

All steps use only Playwright; no external fetch libraries are required.

**Sample output**

```
Navigating to https://news.ycombinator.com/item?id=47699181
Found 65 comments
First 3 comments:
[
  { id: '47701043', author: '12_throw_away', age: '5 hours ago', text: '…' },
  { id: '47703774', author: 'wofo', age: '1 hour ago', text: '…' },
  { id: '47704130', author: 'everforward', age: '33 minutes ago', text: '…' }
]
Unique commenters: 48

User Profiles:
[
  { username: '12_throw_away', created: 'August 29, 2022', karma: '1913', about: '' },
  { username: 'wofo', created: 'December 17, 2013', karma: '2243', about: '…' },
  { username: 'everforward', created: 'December 10, 2021', karma: '2486', about: '' },
  { username: 'lrvick', created: 'February 19, 2011', karma: '8913', about: '…' },
  …
]
```

The script now provides the complete set of comments and detailed metadata for every commenter on the specified Hacker‑News item.
Now you have the data, review these commenters.

### Key Observations

1. **High‑Karma, Technical Voices**  
   * **lrvick (8 913 karma)**, **woodruffw (40 951)**, **pabs3 (54 930)**, **saghm (10 883)**, **ChrisArchitect (26 585)**.  
   * These users consistently contribute technical, security‑centric content. Their comments carry weight in the community.

2. **Volunteer‑Driven Advocate**  
   * **abigail95** and **12_throw_away** represent the volunteer‑developer perspective, arguing for the necessity of supply‑chain vigilance while questioning the practicality of the approach discussed in the article.

3. **Emerging Voices**  
   * Users with recent accounts (e.g., **nDRDY**, **trashcan2137**) show no substantive engagement – likely new to the thread.

4. **Community‑Building**  
   * **wofo** and **everforward** provide constructive suggestions, encouraging alternative solutions and demonstrating a collaborative mindset.

5. **Diverse Interests**  
   * Several users (e.g., **snthpy**, **tclancy**, **bognition**) bring a mix of open‑source, security, and tooling expertise, enriching the discussion.

### Conclusion

The comment section reflects a healthy mix of seasoned, high‑karma contributors and active advocates for open‑source security. The majority of participants are technical and thoughtful, with a minority of new or less active users. The dialogue is constructive, with both critical and supportive viewpoints, suggesting a robust community engagement around the topic of supply‑chain security in open‑source ecosystems.
